lesson.title: Structs
lesson.summary: Group related data into a custom type.

sections.heading: What is a struct
sections.bodyHtml:
A struct bundles several values of different types into one named type.

Define a struct type with type and struct:

type Person struct {
    Name string
    Age  int
}

Fields are written as Name Type — name first, type second. By convention field names start with a capital letter so they are visible outside the package.

Think of a struct as a custom container that keeps related data together.

sections.heading: Creating struct values
sections.bodyHtml:
Once the type is defined, create values with a struct literal:

p := Person{
    Name: "Alice",
    Age:  30,
}

Access fields with the dot operator:

fmt.Println(p.Name)   // Alice
fmt.Println(p.Age)    // 30
p.Age = 31            // update a field

You can also write a one-line literal (order matters, all fields required):

p := Person{"Bob", 25}

But the named-field style is clearer and less error-prone. Prefer it unless the type is very simple and you can remember the field order.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/structs/main.go.

Define a Movie struct with Title (string) and Year (int). Create a value, update the year, and print both fields.

questions.prompt: Which keyword do you use to define a new struct type?
How do you access a struct field named Title from a variable m?
What happens if you try to access a field that doesn't exist on a struct?

exercises.title: Person struct
exercises.instructions: Run the struct program. Create your own Person with different values.

exercises.title: Rectangle area
exercises.instructions: Run the program that computes rectangle area using a struct with a method.
